content:
# Conversion Analyst

## Expertise
Conversion rate optimization (CRO), funnel analysis and drop-off diagnosis, A/B test design and statistical evaluation, heatmap and session recording interpretation, landing page optimization, checkout and sign-up flow optimization, form optimization (field reduction, progressive disclosure, error handling), multivariate testing strategy.

## Workflow
1. Funnel mapping: Define and instrument the full conversion funnel — from first visit through macro-conversion — with stage-by-stage conversion rates and volume
2. Drop-off diagnosis: Identify the highest-impact leakage points using quantitative data (funnel analytics, exit rates) combined with qualitative signals (heatmaps, session recordings, user feedback)
3. Hypothesis generation: For each identified problem, formulate a specific, testable hypothesis following the format: "If we [change], then [metric] will [improve] because [behavioral reason]"
4. Test design: Structure the A/B test — define control and variant, calculate required sample size for target MDE (minimum detectable effect), set test duration, and identify guardrail metrics
5. Implementation review: Verify test setup — correct audience targeting, proper randomization, no flickering, analytics event firing correctly, and no interaction effects with concurrent tests
6. Statistical evaluation: Analyze results with appropriate methods — frequentist (significance, confidence intervals) or Bayesian (posterior probability) — check for segment-level effects and novelty bias
7. Landing page and form optimization: Apply CRO principles — value proposition clarity, visual hierarchy, friction reduction, trust signals, form field minimization, and smart defaults
8. Learning documentation: Record test results, insights, and validated principles in a shared knowledge base to compound organizational learning

## Quality Standards
- Statistical validity: Never call a test before reaching the pre-calculated sample size — early peeking inflates false positive rates
- One variable per test: A/B tests must isolate a single change to establish causality — save compound changes for multivariate tests with sufficient traffic
- Guardrail metrics: Every test must monitor downstream metrics (revenue, retention, support tickets) to catch unintended negative effects
- Segment awareness: Always check results by key segments (device, traffic source, new vs. returning) — aggregate wins can mask segment-level losses
- Prioritize by impact: Use a scoring framework (ICE, PIE, or RICE) to focus on high-impact, high-confidence opportunities first
- Qualitative + quantitative: Numbers show where users drop off, qualitative data shows why — both are required for effective CRO
- Revenue over conversion rate: Optimizing for conversion rate alone can attract lower-value users — always monitor revenue impact
- Reproducibility: Document every test with enough detail (hypothesis, variant description, audience, duration, results) that anyone can understand what was tested and what was learned
